coredata.dc:title: Preparation of biomass carbon dots for foam stabilizer of foamed concrete
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Ning
Li, Zhihe
Wang, Shaoqing
Li, Guo
coredata.dc:description: This paper presents a novel method for preparing an ultra-stable foam based on carbon quantum dots. In this study, carbon quantum dots (RHBCDs) were synthesized using biological waste (rice husk) as carbon source. HRTEM analysis observed the formation of spherical carbon dots with a particle size of no more than 7 nm. The results of FTIR and XPS showed that RHBCDs had good water solubility. XRD analysis confirmed that RHBCDs had typical characteristic peaks of carbon dots. Meanwhile, the performance of foamed concrete prepared by adding RHBCDs to vegetable protein-based surfactant (PS) were studied. The experimental results show that the foams containing RHBCDs have higher density, viscosity and stability. When the addition of RHBCDs was 9.0 wt%, the foam (PS-RS) displacement decreased by 8 % in the first 5 min. PS-RS foam not only reduces the fluidity of foam concrete, but also reduces the hydration exothermic peak of cement. After standard curing, the mechanical properties of foamed concrete increased from 3.38 to 4.64 MPa due to the addition of RHBCDs. And the percentage of pores in the range of 100–500 μm increased to 70.47 %, which means that the addition of RHBCDs not only reduces the pore size distribution and pore size, but also reduces the thermal conductivity. In addition, the shrinkage resistance and homogeneity of foamed concrete have also been improved. In part, one reason is that RHBCDs can be evenly adsorbed on the surface of foam. Another reason is that RHBCDs can fill the pores.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Plant protein-based surfactants
Foamed concrete

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: XRD analysis and fluorescence properties of RHBCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: RHBCDs (a) TEM images; (b) HRTEM images; (c) particle size distribution 3.1.2.FTIR analysis of RHBCDs.
(a) XPS spectra for RHBCDs, (b-d) XPS high resolution spectra.
Micro morphology of foams: (a) PS-20x; (b) PS-30x; (c) PS-RS-20x; (d) PS-RS-30x.
Heat flow of the hydration reactions: (a) The normalized heat flow; (b) The accumulated heat flow.
Foamed concrete without RHBCDs: (a) SEM; (b) EDS.
Foamed concrete with RHBCDs: (a) SEM; (b) EDS.
Two dimensional section of foamed concrete: (a) FC-PS (b) FC-PS-RS.
Foam drainage with different RHBCDs content.

head.ce:title: Preparation of biomass carbon dots for foam stabilizer of foamed concrete

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science
Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Agricultural Engineering and Food Science, Shandong Research Center of Engineering and Technology for Clean Energy, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper presents a novel method for preparing an ultra-stable foam based on carbon quantum dots. In this study, carbon quantum dots (RHBCDs) were synthesized using biological waste (rice husk) as carbon source. HRTEM analysis observed the formation of spherical carbon dots with a particle size of no more than 7 nm. The results of FTIR and XPS showed that RHBCDs had good water solubility. XRD analysis confirmed that RHBCDs had typical characteristic peaks of carbon dots. Meanwhile, the performance of foamed concrete prepared by adding RHBCDs to vegetable protein-based surfactant (PS) were studied. The experimental results show that the foams containing RHBCDs have higher density, viscosity and stability. When the addition of RHBCDs was 9.0 wt%, the foam (PS-RS) displacement decreased by 8 % in the first 5 min. PS-RS foam not only reduces the fluidity of foam concrete, but also reduces the hydration exothermic peak of cement. After standard curing, the mechanical properties of foamed concrete increased from 3.38 to 4.64 MPa due to the addition of RHBCDs. And the percentage of pores in the range of 100–500 μm increased to 70.47 %, which means that the addition of RHBCDs not only reduces the pore size distribution and pore size, but also reduces the thermal conductivity. In addition, the shrinkage resistance and homogeneity of foamed concrete have also been improved. In part, one reason is that RHBCDs can be evenly adsorbed on the surface of foam. Another reason is that RHBCDs can fill the pores.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data/code at the attach file step.

ce:section.ce:para: A large number of scholars have begun to pay attention to carbon dots (CDs) since the discovery of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [1]. CDs is a new type of nanoparticles with fluorescence characteristics, which may be because its surface has functional groups such as oxygen, carboxyl and hydroxyl bonding [2]. CDs has many advantages, such as wide distribution of raw materials, low cost, easy synthesis, non-toxic, excellent fluorescence characteristics, chemical inertia, which makes it have development potential in many fields [3]. Previous studies have confirmed that CDs is an excellent material in many fields, such as biological imaging, photocatalysis, biosensing, drug delivery, metal detection and metal removal [4–6].

There are two approaches to prepare CDs, one is a top-down approach and the other is a bottom-up approach. The top-down approach is to obtain CDs from large carbon molecules [7]. Nevertheless, the bottom-up approach is to extract CDs from biomass materials [8,9]. The top-down approach is usually not the preferred method because of its many shortcomings (complex procedures) [10]. However, the bottom-up approach has the advantages of low cost, abundant raw materials, controllable morphology and simple synthesis method [9,11], especially hydrothermal method is often recommended. Using biological wastes as a green carbon source to prepare carbon quantum dots is not only a friendly way to reduce waste in the environment, but also to find a new carbon source for the synthesis of CDs. In this study, rice husk was selected as the raw material for hydrothermal synthesis of CDs, which is an agricultural waste generated during the rice milling process. The annual output of rice husks is extremely rich, with 156 million tons in 2018 [4]. There are two reasons for the synthesis of CDs using rice husk as a precursor of biological waste. One of them is to convert low-value rice husk waste into high-value raw materials for synthesizing CDs; In addition, the high carbon content of 30–50 % in rice husks is also a reason for the choice [4,12,13].

Additionally, the influence of the CDs as foam stabilizer on the properties of foamed concrete was also investigated. Foamed concrete is created by introducing appropriate foam into cement-based materials [16,17]. Therefore, foamed concrete has the advantages of light weight, high temperature resistance, excellent heat insulation and sound insulation effects [14,15]. The choice of foaming agent has an important influence on the performance of foam concrete. At present, the commonly used foaming agents include protein foaming agent and synthetic foaming agent. The research results of Falliano [15] showed that the foam of protein foaming agent is more uniform and more stable. Foam system belongs to thermodynamic instability, and it will appear the phenomenon of migration fusion drainage under the influence of gravity (foam disproportionation), which is the main reason for the thinning and poor stability of the foam [18–20]. Therefore, enhancing the performance of foamed concrete can be achieved by improving the stability of prefabricated foam [21–23]. Nano solid particles are ideal foam stabilizing materials [24]. Nano solid particles can adsorbed on the pore walls of foam, balancing the cleaning tension of the foam. After nano solid particles are adsorbed on the pore wall of foam, a three-phase foam composed of solid, liquid and gas are formed, which can effectively prevent gas phase transfer and reduce the physical drainage between the gas and liquid phases [25,26]. The results indicated that hydrophobic starch nanoparticles [27], nano-alumina [14], carbon nanotubes particles [25], silica fume particles [28], vermiculite powder [28], granulated blast furnace slag [29], and nano silica particles [22]can effectively improve the performance of foam and foamed concrete. However, so far, there are few reports on the use of CDs as foam stabilizer.

In order to investigate the effects of the CDs on the performances of foam and foamed concrete, plant protein-based surfactants added with CDs (PS-RS) was adopted to produce foamed concrete. Meanwhile, by comparing the properties of the foams without (PS) and with CDs (PS-RS), a novel method for preparing an ultra-stable foam was proposed. Optical microscopy (OM) was employed to study the microstructure of the foam, and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), EDS spectrum and X-ray computed tomography technology were employed to study the performance of the foamed concrete.

Rice husks used as raw material were obtained from the Shengjie Trading Co., Ltd in China. Hydrochloric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Co., Ltd. The plant protein-based surfactants (PS) produced by Tesheng qian New Building Materials Co., ltd., China. The concentration of foaming agent used was 5 %. The Portland cement of P.II 52.5 was used. The specific compositions of cement are shown in Table 1 . All chemicals were not further purified throughout the experiment and deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) used for cleaning and dilution was from PURELAB flex.

First, remove the large impurities in the rice husk, wash it with deionized water, and then use the crusher to make the rice husk into fine particles. In order to remove impurities on the surface of fine particles, it was pretreated with 0.1 M hydrochloric acid, and then repeatedly washed with deionized water until the pH value of the fine particles reached neutrality. The pretreated rice husk fine particles were dried in an oven at 60 ℃ for 24 h. According to previous academic studies [4,30], 190 ℃ was selected as the hydrothermal temperature for the synthesis of CDs in this experiment. The specific operations are as follows: First, 2.0 g of rice husks and 50 mL of deionized water were put into an autoclave lined with Teflon, and hydrothermally treated at 190 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, and then vacuum filtration was carried out to remove excess solids from the mixture. Then, a 0.2 μm cellulose filter was used to obtain a brownish-yellow solution. Finally, small molecules were removed by dialysis in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 3000. The samples were labelled as RHBCDs. The samples were further freeze-dried to obtain CDs powder and stored at 4 ℃.

LED ultraviolet lamp (wavelength of 365 nm) was used to observe the fluorescence emitted by RHBCDs. Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin TEM was used to observe the surface morphology of RHBCDs. FTIR spectroscopy analysis and the XPS were used to determine the elemental composition and surface functional groups of RHBCDs samples. The phase analysis of the sample (XRD) was tested on RIGAKU dmax-3c X-ray powder diffractometer, Cu K α Ray(λ = 0.15418 nm).

Mix PS and water evenly in a container. Then, RHBCDs with different mass percentages (0 wt%, 3.0 wt%, 6.0 wt%, 9.0 wt%, 10.0 wt%, 11.0 wt% and 12.0 wt%) was added to the container. In order to fully dissolve RHBCDs, the mixed solution was stirred at 60 °C for 6 h. After the mixed solution was left standing, foaming was carried out under the pressure of 0.4–0.6 MPa [31]. We labeled the foam prepared with and without RHBCDs to the PS foaming agent as PS and PS-RS, respectively. After the fresh foam was prepared, calculate the density of foam immediately.

The mix of foamed concrete designed in this experiment is shown in Table 2 . The cement content of foamed concrete is generally between 75 and 1400 kg/m3 [17]. In this experiment, the cement content of all samples was 700 kg/m3. According to literature reports [17], the water cement ratio of foamed concrete should be between 0.4 and 1.25. Therefore, the water cement ratio of all samples in this experiment was designed to be 0.5. We usually calculate the mix based on the target density of the foamed concrete because it can be fine-tuned based on the design density of the foamed concrete and the properties of the foam. The formula for the mix design calculation (1 m3 foamed concrete) used in this study is as follows [32]:
(1)
ρ d
=
S a
m c
(2)
V 2
= K
V -
V 1
= K
V -
m c
/
ρ c
+
m w
/
ρ w

where, ρd (kg/m3) is the target density of the foamed concrete. Sa empirical coefficient which is 1.2 for standard 52.5 Portland cement. mc (kg) and mw (kg) is the mass of cement and water separately. V (m3) is the volume of foamed concrete, equal to 1 m3. V1 (m3) and V2 (m3) are the volume of cement paste and foam respectively. ρc (kg/m3) and ρw (kg/m3) are the densities of cement and water. In this paper, mw = 0.5 mc . K is a coefficient, decided by the foam quality.

First, pure cement slurry was stirred at a speed of 100 r/min for half a minute. Then, the foam prepared in advance was added to the cement paste and stirred at the speed of 60–120 r/min for 180 s [33].The mixing time should not be too long, otherwise it will affect the defoaming of foam, the pore structure and macroscopic properties of foamed concrete [17]. Finally, the mixed sample was filled into a mold of 100 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm. After one day, the cured samples were removed from the molds and placed in a standard curing box for 28 days. After 28 days of standard curing, the samples were dried at 60 ℃ for 24 h and then tested for density. The two groups of foamed concrete were marked as FC-PS and FC-PS-RS, respectively. The physical performance tests of the concretes were in compliance with Chinese standard “Foam Concrete” (JG/T 266–2011).

At room temperature, the viscosity and surface tension of foam were measured by rotating viscometer (NDJ-1) [34] and Klux k100 surface tensiometer, respectively. The foam viscosity was tested in a 500 mL beaker using the No. 1 rotor. The lifting ring technology was used to test the surface tension.

The 1 L container was immediately filled with freshly prepared fresh foam, and the foam was weighed within a predetermined time (5, 10, 30, 60, 120, 180, 240, 360, 720, and 1,440 min), and its mass loss was calculated to reflect the stability of foam [22].
Optical microscopy (OM) was used to magnify the foam at 20x and 30x to measure the thickness of the foam walls.

The fluidity of foamed concreteswere measured by the JGJ/T 341–2014 test method. Pour the mixed foamed concrete slurry into an open cylinder with a diameter and height of 80 mm, then slowly remove the barrel to allow the slurry to diffuse freely for 60 s. Finally, measure the diameter of the slurry with a ruler and mark the fluidity of foam concrete. The entire experiment was carried out on an acrylic plate with dimensions of 500 mm × 500 mm.

The hydration heat release rate of foam concrete within 36 h was tested using TAM Air isothermal calorimeter. During the test, the internal temperature of the instrument was always maintained at 20 ± 0.02 ℃.

First, place the cured foamed concrete sample in an oven at 60 °C until the sample quality does not change. The samples were tested for compressive strength based on GB/T 11969–2008 under the condition of constant loading speed of 1 kN/s, respectively.

The water absorption properties of samples were determined according to the method of JGJ/T 341–2014. The specimens cured for 28 days were dried at 60 ℃ to obtain the actual dry weight (md ). Then the mass increase (mw ) of the specimens immersed in water for 24 h was measured, and then the water absorption rate of the sample was calculated by the following formula:
(3)
m w
/
m d
× 100 %

The shrinkage rate of samples were tested according to GB/T 11969–2008. Place the samples with the recorded initial length into the standard curing box, and measure the length of the samples again within a predetermined time (3, 7, 14, 21, 28, 60, and 90 days). The samples size used in this experiment was 40 mm × 40 mm × 160 mm.

Place the well-mixed foam concrete slurry into a cylindrical mold (50 mm in diameter, 1000 mm in height). After standard curing for 28 d, small cylinders with a height of 30 mm were cut out every 250 mm in a cylinder mold and placed in an oven until the quality did not change. The density of the sample was then calculated by measuring the mass and volume of the small cylinder. In this experiment, the following formula was used to evaluate the uniformity of foamed concrete [22]:
(4)
R =
ρ x
ρ 0
Where R is the ratio of the densities, ρx is the density at position ×, and ρ0 represents the density at the top.

The thermal conductivity of foamed concrete was measured according to ASTM C 518 [63]. The measured specimens were the foamed concrete slab with size 300 mm × 300 mm × 30 mm. Before the thermal conductivity was measured, the cured specimens were dried in a 45 vacuum oven until the mass remained unchanged. The thermal conductivity presented in this paper is the average of three specimens and the accuracy is 0.001 W/ (m.k).

The surface morphology of cured foamed concrete was observed by scanning electron microscope of Quanta 250. EDS elemental analysis was detected in the range of Be4–Pu94. Moreover, in order to carry out non-destructive testing on the pore structure of foamed concrete, X-ray computed tomography technology was used.

HRTEM analysis was used to demonstrate the successful synthesis of RHBCDs. From Fig. 1 (a), we can observe that the surface morphology of RHBCDs is spherical. All RHBCDs observed were in amorphous form. From Fig. 1 (b), the lattice stripe spacing of RHBCDs prepared with rice husk as carbon source is 0.25 nm, which corresponds to the crystal plane of graphite (100), and the statistical particle size distribution is about 4.5 nm in Fig. 1 (c). RHBCDs aggregation was also observed. Previous studies [35] have shown that the high energy exists on the surface of RHBCDs, so there is a strong attraction between RHBCDs, leading to the agglomeration of nanoparticles. TEM results showed that the rice husk carbon dots prepared by us had good crystallinity.

As shown in Fig. 2 , we can observe a strong absorption peak at 3300 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum, corresponding to the OH stretching [36]. In addition, a peak corresponding to CO bonding [37] was found at 1640 cm−1 of the spectrum. For RHBCDs, we can observe strong absorption peaks at 1490 cm−1 and 2872 cm−1 respectively [38], which corresponded to the amino functional groups, NH bending and NH stretching. Meanwhile, vibration absorption peak of CN and HC were observed at 1126 cm−1 [39]. FTIR spectra showed that the surfaces of the RHBCDs contained hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino and other functional groups.

The elemental composition and chemical bond states in RHBCDs samples were analyzed using XPS. In Fig. 3 (a), we can observe three main peaks at the binding energies of 285, 400 and 500 eV, respectively, which proves the presence of C1s, N1s and O1s in the RHBCDs samples. At the same time, when the binding energy is 165 eV, there is a small Bi4f peak, indicating that the content of bismuth in the RHBCDs samples is very small. The elemental composition of RHBCDs samples is shown in Table 3 .

The high-resolution XPS spectra were presented in Fig. 3 (b-d). As shown in Fig. 3 (b) that there are basic functional groups at 284.96, 286.53, 287.57, 288.96 and 289.76 eV, which correspond to chemical bonds such as CC, CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively [43,44]. As shown in Fig. 3 (c) that the two peaks at 531.2 and 533.62 eV represents CO and OC-O chemical bonds, respectively [45]. As shown in Fig. 3 (d) that there are CN, CN, and NO chemical bonds at 399.97, 401.68 and 406.47 eV positions [44]. In addition, there are two characteristic peaks in the spectrum, located at 398.7 eV and 400.3 eV, corresponding to pyridine N and pyrrolic N, respectively [46].

The XPS and FTIR results were consistent, both indicated that there were abundant nitrogen and oxygen-containing functions on the surface of RHBCDs. The carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the RHBCDs make the prepared RHBCDs have excellent water solubility [47]. The excellent water solubility makes RHBCDs have potential application prospects in various application fields, which also lays a foundation for further application research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD analysis and fluorescence properties of RHBCDs
ce:section.ce:para: XRD analysis confirmed the structure of the RHBCDs. From the XRD pattern in Fig. 4 , the RHBCDs exhibit a broad peak around 23°, which is a typical characteristic peak of carbon dots. This is consistent with previous reports [40,41].

Fig. 4 (a-c) shows the color of RHBCDs samples under natural light and ultraviolet (UV) light. From Fig. 4(a), it can be seen that when the concentration of RHBCDs is 0.3 mg/mL, it appears brownish yellow under natural light. However, under UV light, when the concentration of RHBCDs increased from 0.3 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL, the fluorescent color deepens from light green to light blue. According to the XPS experimental results, RHBCDs contain a small amount of bismuth (Bi), which may be the reason for the higher luminous intensity. Scholars [4,42] have demonstrated that bismuth can lead to a reduction in the band gap of the RHBCDs, resulting in a slight red shift of the peak to the visible region.

Table 4 presents the effect of RHBCDs content on the bubble stabilization time of foam. From the data presented in Table 4, we concluded that the bubble stabilization time increased from 0.0 wt% RHBCDs to 9.0 wt% RHBCDs, and also decreased at 10.0 wt% RHBCDs. When the concentration of RHBCDs on the surface of foam liquid membrane increases, more of the space originally belonging to the surfactant was occupied by the RHBCDs, resulting in an uneven distribution of the surfactant concentration in the liquid film. Partial increases in concentration can lead to the formation of micelles and even vesicles, which reduces the functionality of the surfactant and ultimately reduces the stability of the foam [48]. Therefore, the optimum addition amount of RHBCDs after optimization is 9.0 wt%.

Table 5 illustrates the effect of RHBCDs on the performance of foams. After adding RHBCDs to PS, the density of foam increased by 39 kg/m3 and the viscosity of foam increased by 0.047 Pa.S. At the same time, the surface tension of the foam was reduced by 13.3 mN/m. Previous studies have shown that the higher the viscosity of foam, the more favorable to improve the strength of liquid film and the stability of foam [44].

As shown in Fig. 5 , when RHBCDs were added to the foam, the water displacement of the foam dropped by 8 % in the first 5 min. We can infer from previous studies [21] that when RHBCDs is combined with vegetable protein-based surfactants, they can play a role in improving foam stability.

As shown in Fig. 6 , after RHBCDs were added to foam, the thickness of liquid film increased by 38.55 μm. This is because RHBCDs were uniformly adsorbed on the foam liquid film, which improves the thickness and strength of foam at the same time, thus delaying the drainage time of foam. In short, the overall performance of the foam was improved when RHBCDs were used as foam stabilizers.

The fluidity diagrams of the two groups of foamed concrete are shown in Fig. 7 . We can observe that the fluidity of the two groups is 197 mm and 189 mm, respectively. After the addition of RHBCDs, the flow ability of foamed concrete was reduced by 8 mm. Previous studies [23] have shown that foam with high viscosity and low drainage is beneficial to reduce the fluidity of foamed concrete.

Isothermal calorimetry was used to study the hydration heat of two samples. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the dormancy period and main peak of the two groups of samples appear at about 2 h and 10 h, respectively. As the RHBCDs may absorb water in the early mixing stage of foam concrete, the height of the main peak decreases slightly. Part of the water is stored in the RHBCDs to reduce the effective water binder ratio in the system, thereby reducing the hydration exothermic peak of the cement [49]. It can be seen from Fig. 8 (b) that the cumulative heat generated by the sample after RHBCDs addition was lower than the control group, again confirms the above views [59,60].

Fig. 9 illustrates the effect of adding RHBCDs to foamed concrete on mechanical properties. With the increase of curing time, the mechanical property of both groups of samples increased. However, it can be seen from the figure that with the addition of RHBCDs, the 7 d compressive strength of samples decreases, which may be caused by the reduction of thermal evolution [50,59,60]. After curing to the 28th day, the mechanical property of samples increased by 1.26 MPa, which may be related to internal curing and enhanced foam stability. Therefore, we can conclude that the addition of RHBCDs is beneficial to improve the mechanical property of samples.

Foamed concrete prepared with RHBCDs has the same optimal performance as other solid wastes (nano alumina [14], lime mud fly ash [56], wet-milled waste limestone powder [57]). We also found that the compressive strength of foamed concrete with rice husk was slightly higher than that of previous work [14,27]. One reason is that the RHBCDs can uniformly adsorb on the surface of foam to increase the thickness of foam wall, thereby improving the strength of foam and foamed concrete. Moreover, the content of silicon dioxide in rice husk is high [61]. Silicon dioxide has the effect of inducing hydration and pozzolanic activity, which accelerates the process of hydration and generates more hydration products, thus significantly improving its mechanical strength [62]. At the same time, rice husk is a widely distributed biomass material. Another advantage of rice husks is that they are readily available, inexpensive [58], non-polluting and sustainable. Therefore, this paper explores new application areas of rice husks.

The drying shrinkage of the two groups of samples is shown in Fig. 10 . On the 90th day, the dry shrinkage of the specimens decreased by 0.95 mm/m due to the addition of RHBCDs to the foamed concrete. It shows that the introduction of RHBCDs is beneficial to improve the shrinkage resistance of samples. This may be due to the addition of RHBCDs, which makes the internal moisture of foamed concrete difficult to evaporate in a dry environment, and increases the resistance to drying shrinkage, so the drying shrinkage decreases [14].

After standard curing, the effect of RHBCDs on the water absorption of foamed concrete is shown in Fig. 11 . After adding RHBCDs, the water absorption of samples decreased by 13.1 %. This indicates that RHBCDs are beneficial to reduce the water absorption of foamed concrete. Foam with the highest viscosity (PS-RS) is beneficial to reduce the coalescence and drainage of foam, and also to optimize the pore size of samples. So as to improve the mechanical property of samples, to achieve the purpose of reducing the water absorption of samples [21]. Kearsley [51] once proposed that the general water absorption of foamed concrete can be explained by its matrix. Therefore, RHBCDs can fill the capillary pores of foamed concrete, thereby reducing the water absorption of foamed concrete [22].

Stability test is very important for testing the performance of samples. Because when the bubble is in the transition stage between fresh state and coagulation and hardening, instability will occur, which will lead to the loss of air in the slurry, and in severe cases will lead to the collapse of the entire system [27]. After mixing, the material is evenly poured into the column without leaving any macro bubbles in the process. After 24 h, the drop levels of the control group and the experimental group were measured by laser displacement sensor. The average decline of the control group samples was 1.928 mm, while the average decline of the experimental group samples was 1.206 mm. This result highlights the beneficial effect of RHBCDs on the stability of foamed concrete. RHBCDs can play a beneficial role in resisting the instability phenomenon widely existing in foam concrete.

Fig. 12 illustrates the consistency of two groups of foamed concrete. As shown in the figure, the density of both groups of samples decreases with the increase of test height. However, the addition of RHBCDs reduced the ratio of bottom density to top density from 1.26 times to 1.03 times. In conclusion, RHBCDs are beneficial to improve the homogeneity of samples.

Compared with the previous related research and Chinese standard JGJ/T341-2014 [64] (as shown in Fig. 13 ), the thermal conductivity of FC-PS-RS was reduced by 30 % with the same density grade. This may be because the addition of RHBCDs makes the pore distribution of foam concrete more uniform, smaller and more concentrated. Previous studies have shown that this pore distribution will reduce the thermal conductivity [65].

The micro morphology of samples are shown in Figs. 14 and 15
. There are a lot of cracks in the holes in the sample before RHBCDs were added. When RHBCDs were added, the pores of the samples became smooth and complete. She et al. [22] discovered that the higher the hydration degree of the sample, the more uniform the distribution of hydration products, the greater the strength of the cell wall, and the better the overall performance of samples. The experimental results show that the addition of RHBCDs is beneficial to improve the hydration degree of samples.

In the EDS spectrum, we found that the calcium silicon (Ca/Si) ratio of the sample was significantly reduced after the addition of RHBCDs. Previous studies [52] have shown that low Ca/Si ratio can inhibit the alkali aggregate reaction. Therefore, appropriate addition of RHBCDs is beneficial to improve the durability of samples [53].

After standard curing, scan two groups of samples with a scanner, and the two-dimensional and three-dimensional pictures are shown in Figs. 16 and 17
. The pore size frequency distribution of the sample was processed as shown in Fig. 18 . According to previous studies [54], the pore size distribution of foamed concrete is logarithmic.

The pore size ratio of f samples in the size range of 100–500 μm increased from 8.04 % to 70.47 %. This may be because RHBCDs are uniformly adsorbed on the pore wall of foam, reducing the aggregation and physical drainage of the foam, and ultimately improving the uniformity of the sample [22].

Porosity is an important parameter in the study of cement-based materials. When the density of foam concrete is the same, its porosity is usually considered to be similar. Therefore, the foamed concrete may have the same density but different properties due to the combined effect of the pore structure and the matrix microstructure [14]. After adding RHBCDs, the pore size distribution of the sample becomes narrower, the pore size becomes smaller, and the strength of the pore wall increases, indicating that RHBCDs are beneficial to improve the performance of samples [55].

This study proposes a novel approach of preparing carbon quantum dots from rice husk as a foam stabilizer. The following are the specific experimental results:
1.
HRTEM analysis can confirm that rice husk can be successfully prepared into carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method. Due to its excellent water solubility, RHBCDs can be used as foam stabilizers.
2.
The density, bubble wall, and foam stability can be significantly increased with the RHBCDs as foam stabilizer. This is beneficial to delaying the drainage of foam and improving the strength of foam.
3.
The addition of RHBCDs to foam can reduce the fluidity of foamed concrete and the hydration exothermic peak of cement. This can improve the shrinkage resistance of foam concrete.
4.
X-CT analysis revealed that ultra-light foamed concrete with uniform pore distribution, small pore size, and concentrated pore size distribution hadlower thermal conductivity. The foamed concrete with RHBCDs as foam stabilizer possess the narrower pore size distribution, which may be the reason for improving the strength and uniformity of foamed concrete.

Ning Song: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Software, Writing – original draft. Zhihe Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Resources, Funding acquisition. Shaoqing Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Software. Guo Li: Conceptualization, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2019YFD1100600), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52176192, 52130610), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2021ME035, ZR2021QE132).
